training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to understand and follow a basic 5-step lesson plan that includes one measurable learning objective.
training_sequence.rationale: Root fix for vague or absent goals. If no measurable objective is stated, lesson planning is the foundational gap. Same video serves SI2 (no opening) and PIC-1 (no alignment). If already shown for either, skip to Obs 4.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn how to use quick checks for understanding (e.g., exit tickets, thumbs-up/thumbs-down) to gauge student comprehension during lessons.
training_sequence.rationale: Once planning habit is forming, the next SI1 failure is absent comprehension checks. This LO directly builds that habit.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to identify and describe the six levels of Bloom's Taxonomy with examples of classroom tasks at each level.
training_sequence.rationale: If SI1 still failing after planning + checking, the issue is usually vague objective verbs. Bloom's gives the teacher the language to write measurable goals.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to understand and follow a basic 5-step lesson plan that includes one measurable learning objective.
training_sequence.rationale: No lesson architecture = no logical flow. The 5-step plan establishes opening–middle–end as a structural habit. Same video as SI1 Obs 3 and PIC-1 Obs 3. If already shown, skip to Obs 4.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to sequence 2–3 connected lessons using progressive learning objectives and varied strategies for concept development.
training_sequence.training_title: Using Varied Strategies to Build Connected Lessons
training_sequence.rationale: If flow is still poor after planning is addressed, multi-lesson sequencing forces deliberate transition design across a unit — making coherence a planned act.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to Demonstrate understanding of number sense up to 100 using place value models and bundling techniques (tens frames, number lines).
training_sequence.rationale: Number/operations errors — most common Gr 1–3 math content failure. AI matches to topic-specific LO from transcript.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to identify and teach individual letter sounds, vowels, blends, digraphs (e.g., sh, ch) using phonics-based strategies.
training_sequence.rationale: Phonics errors — most common early English content failure. AI matches to topic from transcript.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to recognize and correctly pronounce all Urdu letters (الف سے ی) with proper articulation, including bhari and halki awaazain.
training_sequence.rationale: Letter/sound errors — most common early Urdu content failure. AI matches to topic from transcript.

training_sequence.training_title: ⚑ GAP: No science content LOs exist
training_sequence.rationale: Confirmed gap. Escalate to coach through nudge

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to understand and follow a basic 5-step lesson plan that includes one measurable learning objective.
training_sequence.rationale: Alignment fails first because there is no planning habit linking objective to activity. Same video as SI1 Obs 3. If already shown, skip to Obs 4.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to identify and describe the six levels of Bloom's Taxonomy with examples of classroom tasks at each level.
training_sequence.rationale: If basic planning is forming but cognitive level is mismatched (topic right, Bloom's level wrong), Bloom's gives the framework to align task demand to objective verb.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to create a basic 5-step lesson plan using one measurable learning objective and an appropriate activity for concept engagement.
training_sequence.rationale: If alignment still fails, this L1 plan LO requires the teacher to actively design alignment — producing a plan where objective and activity are explicitly matched.

training_sequence.training_title: ⚑ GAP: No Academic Language LO exists
training_sequence.rationale: Confirmed gap. Escalate to coach through nudge

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to diagnose and correct common misconceptions in basic operations and number sense (e.g., borrowing, carrying errors).
training_sequence.rationale: Most direct match for math misconception handling. Gives teacher a taxonomy of common errors and correct responses.

training_sequence.training_title: ⚑ GAP: No misconception diagnosis LO exists for English or Urdu literacy
training_sequence.rationale: Confirmed gap. Escalate to coach through nudge

training_sequence.domain: Science in Personal and Social Perspective
training_sequence.training_lo: Explores the prevalent misconceptions and incorrect beliefs that students may hold regarding scientific concepts.
training_sequence.rationale: Science misconceptions - part 1

training_sequence.domain: Science in Personal and Social Perspective
training_sequence.training_lo: Aiming to address and correct these misunderstandings through targeted instruction and clarification.
training_sequence.rationale: Science misconceptions - part 2

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use open-ended questions to encourage critical thinking and student expression during lessons.
training_sequence.rationale: Entry point for all questioning failures. Provides question stems and the conceptual case for open questions. Must come before any other questioning skill.

training_sequence.training_lo: Use Socratic questioning to engage students in inquiry-based learning (e.g., 'How does climate change affect local ecosystems?').
training_sequence.rationale: If teacher asks open questions but never probes deeper after a student responds, Socratic questioning builds the follow-up as the core questioning move.

training_sequence.training_lo: Use Bloom's Taxonomy to create 3 levels of questions for a lesson (e.g., recall, application, analysis for addition problems).
training_sequence.rationale: If questioning quality still poor, designing questions in advance at multiple cognitive levels makes deliberate probing a planned act.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn to provide positive verbal feedback to students, including simple suggestions for improvement (e.g., 'Great job on your math problems!' or 'Well done on reading the passage aloud.').
training_sequence.rationale: Most direct match. Provides explicit language templates for specific, actionable feedback. Entry point for both no-feedback and generic-praise failures.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn to use formative assessments like quizzes, small group discussions, and written reflections to measure student understanding and identify knowledge gaps.
training_sequence.rationale: If feedback remains generic, teacher may lack diagnostic skill — cannot give specific feedback without identifying what is right or wrong. Formative assessment builds this.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to implement diagnostic assessments at the beginning of lessons to assess prior knowledge on a topic (e.g., pre-test on fractions or a set of questions to assess understanding of previous topics).
training_sequence.rationale: Most direct match. Teacher does not elicit prior knowledge at all. Builds the habit of checking before teaching.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to design diagnostic assessments at the beginning of each unit to assess students' prior knowledge, ensuring alignment with unit goals and objectives.
training_sequence.rationale: If PIA-1 still failing, teacher needs to plan the prior knowledge bridge in advance — not improvise it. Unit-level diagnostic design builds this as a planning habit.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use real-life stories and multiple representations to teach word problems in all four operations.
training_sequence.rationale: Math only at this specificity. Real-life story LO is the right entry for connection-making in math — concrete and immediately applicable.

training_sequence.training_lo: This activity can be conducted as per teachers' choice
training_sequence.training_title: Practicing different Student Centered Activities- Backpack full of memories
training_sequence.rationale: Literacy connection building through student-centered activities

training_sequence.training_lo: This activity can bring excitement for the learners!
training_sequence.training_title: Examples of Activities in Teaching - Backpack full of Memories
training_sequence.rationale: More literacy connection examples

training_sequence.training_lo: Design and facilitate an inquiry-based learning experience driven by a real-world question.
training_sequence.rationale: If PIA-2 still failing, inquiry learning makes real-world connection non-optional — every lesson starts from a real-world question by design.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn how to scaffold lessons into smaller, manageable steps for students who struggle with complex concepts.
training_sequence.rationale: Most achievable differentiation in large classrooms — verbal adjustments only, no extra materials. Direct match for no-scaffolding failure.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn to incorporate Universal Design for Learning (UDL), providing multiple means of representation (videos, audios, visuals) to cater to various learning needs.
training_sequence.training_title: Universal Design for Learning: Teaching for Every Child
training_sequence.rationale: If scaffolding habit is not forming, UDL provides the planning framework for designing lessons for diversity from the start.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to design and assign differentiated instructional activities, including tailored lessons and homework tasks, based on students' individual learning profiles and needs.
training_sequence.training_title: Design and Assign differentiated Instructional Activities
training_sequence.rationale: If PIA-3 still failing, teacher needs to move from verbal scaffolding to structured task differentiation.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to learn to provide differentiated assessments (e.g., written tests, oral presentations, peer reviews) to meet diverse student needs.
training_sequence.rationale: If differentiation still absent at assessment stage, this LO addresses it — different ways to demonstrate learning.

training_sequence.training_title: ⚑ GAP: No LO addresses building alternative explanation strategies
training_sequence.rationale: Confirmed gap. No dedicated training exists for alternative explanation techniques (analogy, diagram, different example). Escalate to coach through nudge

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to implement student-centered instructional strategies (e.g., group work, peer teaching, classroom routines) effectively in overcrowded classrooms to maintain engagement, manage behavior, and enhance learning outcomes.
training_sequence.rationale: Directly addresses the core fear: student ownership = classroom chaos. Demonstrates it is compatible with large-class management.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to implement peer teaching where students explain concepts to each other.
training_sequence.rationale: Peer teaching gives students genuine ownership of explaining content — structured and low-risk.

training_sequence.training_lo: Facilitate student-led discussions to assign roles and responsibilities in group projects.
training_sequence.rationale: Roles and responsibilities give the teacher a protocol for handing over control — structured enough to feel safe.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to set a clear, actionable vision for the classroom, communicating expectations and goals, and involving students in co-creating the classroom culture.
training_sequence.rationale: If PIA-5 still failing, the issue is the teacher's fundamental belief about student role. Co-creating culture gives students formal ownership.

training_sequence.training_lo: What is GRR? Graphical representation of it's implementation in classrooms which clearly defines the role of teachers and students
training_sequence.rationale: Understanding the GRR model

training_sequence.training_lo: How does a teacher makes students independent learners?
training_sequence.rationale: Components of the GRR model for student independence

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to diagnose and correct common misconceptions in basic operations and number sense (e.g., borrowing, carrying errors).
training_sequence.rationale: No dedicated math discourse LO exists. Misconceptions LO is the best entry — gives a practical reason to ask 'why does that work?' — first step toward reasoning discourse.

training_sequence.training_lo: Use Socratic questioning to engage students in inquiry-based learning.
training_sequence.rationale: Closest available to math reasoning discourse — probing follow-ups after student responses.

training_sequence.training_lo: Design tasks that require students to generate and compare multiple approaches to a problem.
training_sequence.training_title: Encouraging Multiple Solutions in the Classroom
training_sequence.rationale: Multiple solution approaches IS the core of mathematical discourse. Directly builds the 'did anyone solve it differently?' habit.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use open-ended questions to encourage critical thinking and student expression during lessons.
training_sequence.rationale: No productive-struggle LO exists. Open-ended questioning introduces the value of student thinking time — first step toward tolerating productive difficulty.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use strategies such as Think-Pair-Share and brainstorming to promote critical thinking, creative idea generation, and meaningful student discussion in every lesson.
training_sequence.training_title: ہر سبق میں تنقیدی سوچ اور تخلیقی صلاحیتیں: پاکستانی اساتذہ کے لیے ایک عملی رہنما
training_sequence.rationale: TPS structures struggle time — students grapple in pairs before sharing, giving the teacher a management framework for productive struggle.

training_sequence.training_lo: Design and facilitate small-group problem-solving tasks with peer accountability and shared outcomes.
training_sequence.rationale: Small-group problem-solving embeds productive struggle as a designed experience — teacher becomes facilitator, not answer-provider.

training_sequence.training_lo: Design and facilitate an inquiry-based learning experience driven by a real-world question.
training_sequence.rationale: No L0/L1 LO addresses inquiry sequence. L2 is the only direct match — it structurally requires starting with a question, preventing the teacher from giving the answer first.

training_sequence.training_lo: Design and facilitate small-group problem-solving tasks with peer accountability and shared outcomes.
training_sequence.rationale: If inquiry design is still not forming, structured small-group problem-solving gives a safer format — students grapple with a scenario together before teacher explains.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use open-ended questions to encourage critical thinking and student expression during lessons.
training_sequence.rationale: Stepping back: if L2 inquiry is not landing, the prerequisite skill of open questioning may be missing. Build from L1.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to use strategies such as Think-Pair-Share and brainstorming to promote critical thinking, creative idea generation, and meaningful student discussion in every lesson.
training_sequence.training_title: ہر سبق میں تنقیدی سوچ اور تخلیقی صلاحیتیں: پاکستانی اساتذہ کے لیے ایک عملی رہنما
training_sequence.rationale: No science-talk LO exists. TPS is the most effective structural break from pure IRE — students discuss science ideas in pairs before sharing.

training_sequence.training_lo: Facilitate student-led discussions to assign roles and responsibilities in group projects.
training_sequence.rationale: Student-led discussion removes teacher from the centre of talk — closest available for a dialogic science classroom.

training_sequence.training_lo: Design and facilitate small-group problem-solving tasks with peer accountability and shared outcomes.
training_sequence.rationale: Small-group science tasks create structured science talk by necessity — students must discuss observations and conclusions together.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to identify and teach individual letter sounds, vowels, blends, digraphs (e.g., sh, ch) using phonics-based strategies.
training_sequence.rationale: No pedagogical workaround substitutes for phonics knowledge. Most direct and only appropriate entry for English.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to model phoneme segmentation and blending techniques using everyday classroom examples (e.g., /k/ /æ/ /t/ = cat).
training_sequence.rationale: If sounds are taught but blending is absent, this LO specifically addresses the blending and segmentation methodology — distinct from sound-letter knowledge.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to identify the key components of phonological awareness (e.g., vowels, phonics and CVC words).
training_sequence.rationale: If phonics instruction still incomplete, phonological awareness as a broader framework gives the teacher the conceptual map of how sounds work in English.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to teach Harakaat (زیر، زبر، پیش) and their effect on pronunciation and meaning through guided practice.
training_sequence.rationale: Harakaat IS Urdu phonics — equivalent of sound-letter correspondence. Entry point for Urdu phonics instruction.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to conduct Arkaan Saazi lessons (دو حرفی اور تین حرفی ارکان) to help children blend sounds into basic words.
training_sequence.rationale: Urdu blending — equivalent of English blending methodology. Distinct skill from harakaat.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to ask comprehension questions before, during, and after reading to support meaning-making.
training_sequence.rationale: Entry point. Teacher likely only asks post-reading questions. Before / during / after questioning builds comprehension as an ongoing process, not a post-reading test.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to explicitly teach comprehension strategies (predicting, inferring, summarizing, visualizing) and monitor their use during reading.
training_sequence.rationale: If before/during/after questioning is forming but strategy instruction is absent, this LO addresses the qualitative shift — from asking questions to teaching the thinking process behind them.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to connect vocabulary instruction to reading and writing tasks using word webs, semantic maps, and context applications.
training_sequence.training_title: Vocabulary Instructions to Reading and Writing
training_sequence.rationale: If comprehension instruction still weak, vocabulary–reading integration is the next layer — students comprehend better when vocabulary is taught in reading context.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to analyze and plan multi-skill literacy lessons that integrate reading, writing, and oral language.
training_sequence.rationale: Most direct match. Builds the habit of designing reading and writing as a connected sequence. Applicable across English and Urdu.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to connect vocabulary instruction to reading and writing tasks using word webs, semantic maps, and context applications.
training_sequence.training_title: Vocabulary Instructions to Reading and Writing
training_sequence.rationale: Vocabulary as a thread connecting reading and writing gives a concrete linking mechanism — words encountered in reading become words used in writing.

training_sequence.training_lo: Teacher will be able to incorporate listening texts and oral narratives into literacy lessons to build auditory comprehension.
training_sequence.training_title: Integrating listening texts and oral narratives into literacy lessons to build auditory comprehension
training_sequence.rationale: Listening → discussing → writing is the fullest literacy integration. Adds oral language as a third strand reinforcing the reading-writing connection.
